answer:
**Iqbal's poetry in the Falsafa corpus—specifically *Bang-e-Dara* (1924), available in three parts—references 'wahdat' (wahdat, oneness/unity) in a poetic, mystical sense, tied to an underlying divine reality manifesting amid diversity (kasrat, multiplicity).[^1][^2] It appears in transliterations, rendered in English translation as "Oneness" or "Unity." The corpus lacks Iqbal's prose works (e.g., *Asrar-e-Khudi* or *The Reconstruction of Religious Thought in Islam*), so no systematic philosophical treatise on wahdat al-wujud (unity of being) or its critique is present; these poems evoke it allusively.[^3]**

### Wahdat as Underlying Unity in Multiplicity
In "Jugnu" (The Firefly, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 27), wahdat is the primal, hidden oneness whose "secret... has become concealed in multiplicity" (kasrat mein... wahdat ka raaz makhfi). All created things are "traces" of "Primal Beauty," drawn "from solitude [unity] to sight [diverse manifestation]":

> A hidden glimpse of **Primal Beauty's light**,  
> Which **Nature brought from solitude to sight**.[^4]

The poem unifies phenomena—firefly's glow, flower's scent, bird's song—as expressions of this Beauty:
> **Of Primal Beauty, every thing's a trace**:  
> In man, it's speech; in buds, a bursting grace.[^5]

It culminates: "**In all the Many, Unity lies deep**; / The firefly's shine is scent the flowers keep." Distinctions are illusory; wahdat endures beneath.[^6]

This echoes a cycle: primal solitude (wahdat) → Nature's manifestation in kasrat → recognition of enduring unity.

### Other Mentions
- *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 26 ("Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet"): Guru Nanak "sang the **song of Oneness**" (wahdat ka geet) in India, linking it to sacred history.[^7]
- *Bang-e-Dara Part 2*, ch. 6: "Perfection of **oneness** is manifest" (kamaal-e-wahdat ayaan), in a visionary context.[^8]

No explicit "cycle" (e.g., ego's ascent via khudi/selfhood) appears; the corpus has no matches for "cycle," "selfhood," "khudi," or English equivalents like "unity" beyond these.[^9] Iqbal's fuller dynamic ontology—wahdat as affirming selfhood, not dissolution—is absent here.

[^1]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 27 (*Jugnu*), translation: /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/27-jugnu/translation/.
[^2]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 26 (*Hindustani Bachon Ka Qaumi Geet*), transliteration search hit: /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/transliteration/#p-c3c262.
[^3]: Corpus metadata confirms only *Bang-e-Dara* Parts 1–3 (Urdu poetry); no philosophical prose.
[^4]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 27, [p-81ff7c](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/27-jugnu/translation/#p-81ff7c).
[^5]: Ibid., [p-86d8dc](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/27-jugnu/translation/#p-86d8dc).
[^6]: Ibid., [p-24c42c](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/27-jugnu/translation/#p-24c42c); cf. [p-2290ee](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/27-jugnu/translation/#p-2290ee) on human illusion.
[^7]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 26, [p-da6786](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/26-hindustani-bachon-ka-qaumi-geet/translation/#p-da6786).
[^8]: *Bang-e-Dara Part 2*, ch. 6, transliteration: /works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/06-zamana-dekhe-ga-jab-mere-dil-se-mahshar-uthe-ga-gu/transliteration/.
[^9]: Searches for "unity," "oneness," "cycle," "selfhood"/khudi/ego yielded 0 English results beyond poetry hits.
